sections.summary: Combining lisinopril with spironolactone can cause dangerous hyperkalemia, especially in patients with renal impairment, diabetes, or advanced age.
sections.mechanism: Lisinopril reduces aldosterone by blocking angiotensin converting enzyme, decreasing potassium excretion. Spironolactone independently blocks aldosterone receptors in the distal nephron. Together they additively raise serum potassium.
sections.clinical_effects: Hyperkalemia that may be asymptomatic until severe, when it can cause muscle weakness, cardiac conduction abnormalities, and life-threatening arrhythmias.
sections.management: Use the combination cautiously with a clear indication such as heart failure. Start with low doses, avoid potassium supplements and salt substitutes, and correct volume depletion.
sections.monitoring: Check serum potassium and renal function within one week of starting or adjusting either drug, then periodically thereafter.

evidence.citation: Juurlink DN, et al. N Engl J Med. 2004;351(6):543-551
